content.full_text:
State of California

Fair Political Practices Commission
P.O. BOX 807 • SACRAMENTO, 95804 • • • 1100 K STREET BUILDING, SACRAMENTO, 95814

Technical Assistance          Administration          Executive Legal          Enforcement          Conflict of Interest
(916) 322-5662          322-5660          322-5901          322-5441          322-5444

May 14, 1980


Robert M. Hinrichs
Abramson, Church & Stave
Third Floor, Crocker Bank Building          A-80-05-026
Salinas, CA 93901

Re: Opinion Request
     No. 80-003

Dear Mr. Hinrichs:

Thank you for your letter of March 27, 1980, requesting
an opinion regarding the campaign disclosure provisions of
the Political Reform Act. Because your question does not
raise a significant question of interpretation regarding the
Political Reform Act no formal opinion will be issued in
response to it. Your specific question involves whether a
corporation may provide transportation to candidates on
corporation aircraft and not incur a reporting obligation if
the candidate is charged for the flight pursuant to the
schedule of allowable charges under FAA Regulation 91.131.

The purpose of the Political Reform Act is to provide
information about the sources of financial support of political
candidates so that the voters may properly evaluate what
interests the candidate is likely to represent or to which
he might be beholden. As the Supreme Court said in Buckley
v. Valeo (1976) 424 U.S. 1, 66:

[d]isclosure provides the electorate with information
"as to where political campaign money comes from and
how it is spent by the candidate" in order to aid the
voters in evaluating those who seek ... office. It
allows voters to place each candidate in the political
spectrum more precisely than is often possible solely
on the basis of party labels and campaign speeches.
Second, disclosure requirements deter actual corruption
and avoid the appearance of corruption by exposing
large contributions and expenditures to the light of
publicity.

Robert M. Hinrichs
May 14, 1980
Page Two


In keeping with the purpose of the Act, therefore, disclosure
must be made whenever services are provided to a candidate
for which full and adequate consideration is not received,
since such provision of services is a form of financial
support for the candidate.

Turning to your specific question, the provision of air
transportation for a candidate in connection with his or her
corporation's private plane is reportable as a contribution
to the extent that the candidate pays less than the fair
market value for his flight. In the situation you described,
the fair market value is either the maximum allowable charge
per passenger under FAA Regulation, Part. 91.181, or the
cost of purchasing equivalent service from a commercial air
line, whichever is greater. If the candidate pays any less
than the fair market value he has received a "discount or
rebate not available to the public generally," which constitutes
a contribution. Government Code Section 82015.

In a subsequent telephone call you also asked what the
corporation has to report when its private aircraft is used
by a full-time lobbyist employed by the corporation. The
travel expenses of the lobbyist should be reported as "other
payments to influence legislative or administrative action"
under 2 Cal. Adm. Code Section 18620(d). The maximum amount
which may be charged under FAA Regulation Part. 91.181 is an
appropriate amount to report for the cost of the flight
under 2 Cal. Adm. Code Section 18620(d)(2). If the pilot or
any other employee spends more than 10 percent of his or her
compensated time providing travel services for lobbyists, a
report may also be required under 2 Cal. Adm. Code Section
18620(d)(1).

I trust this answers your questions. If you wish to
appeal the denial of your opinion request, you may do so
pursuant to 2 Cal. Adm. Code Section 18321. If you have any
questions, please contact Barbara Milman of the Commission's
Legal Division.

Sincerely,

Ted Prim
Executive Director

TP:BM:plh

LAW OFFICES

HARRISON, CHURCH & STAVE

SIDNEY L. CHURCH (1909-1952)
JACOB HARRISON (1909-1975)
ANDREW CHURCH
JOSEPH A. STAVE
LLOYD W. DINKELSPIEL
ROBERT A. HASTINGS
JOHN G. STEWART
ARNOLD B. MYERS
RICHARD B. ARLEDSON
PAUL D. GULLION

THE CLOCK BUILDING
560 MAIN STREET

SALINAS, CALIFORNIA 93901

Request No. 80,003
Date Received 5-31-80

Response Due 4/14-80

do Levemen.

March 27, 1980

Fair Political Practices Commission
State of California
P.O. Box 807
Sacramento, CA 95804

Re: Use of Corporate Aircraft in Political Campaigns

Gentlemen:

It is the purpose of this letter to request
a formal opinion on the valuation of the use of corporation
aircraft for transportation in connection with state and
local political campaigns. We understand that if the
corporation makes contributions in excess of $3,000.00 in
connection with all candidates and all ballot measures for
a particular election, it must report those contributions
to your commission. The question arises as to how to
evaluate in kind contributions for the use of a corporate
aircraft in transporting candidates.

Under the Federal Aviation Regulations, Part 91.181,
there is authority for the operator of an aircraft not
licensed as a common carrier, to make the following charges
to certain "guests" of the company under a "time sharing
agreement":

"(d) The following may be charged, as expenses
of a specific flight, for transportation as authorized
by paragraphs (b)(3) and (7) and (c)(1) of this
section:
(1) Fuel, oil, lubricants, and other additives.
(2) Travel expenses of the crew, including food,
lodging, and ground transportation.
(3) Hangar and tie-down costs away from the
aircraft's base of operations.
(4) Insurance obtained for the specific flight.
(5) Landing fees, airport taxes, and similar
assessments.
(6) Customs, foreign permit, and similar fees directly
related to the flight.

Fair Political Practices Commission
Page 2
March 27, 1980

     (7) In flight food and beverages.
     (8) Passenger ground transportation.
     (9) Flight planning and weather contract services
     (10) An additional charge equal to 100 percent of the
expenses listed in subparagraph (1) of this
paragraph."

     Our specific question is this: If the operator
of the aircraft actually charges the candidate and is paid
for the above items, would it be justified in taking the
position that it had received full and adequate consideration
for transportation so that it would not have to report
a contribution?

     If you need any further information on this,
please give me a call.


                                        Very truly yours,

                                        [signature]
                                        ROBERT M. HINRICHS
                                        For ABRAMSON, CHURCH & STAVE

Hinrichs Abramson, Church & Stave Third Floor, Crocker Bank Building Salinas, CA 93901 Re: Opinion Request No. 80-003 Dear Mr. Hinrichs: Thank you for your letter of March 27, 1980, requesting an opinion regarding the campaign disclosure provisions of the Political Reform Act. Because your question does not raise a significant question of interpretation regarding the Political Reform Act no formal opinion will be issued in response to it. Your specific question involves whether a corporation may provide transportation to candidates on corporation aircraft and not incur a reporting obligation if the candidate is charged for the flight pursuant to the schedule of allowable charges under FAA Regulation 91.181. The purpose of the Political Reform Act is to provide information about the sources of financial support of political candidates so that the voters may properly evaluate what interests the candidate is likely to represent or to which he might be beholden. As the Supreme Court said in Buckley v. Valeo (1976) 424 U.S. 1, 66: [d]isclosure provides the electorate with information "as to where political campaign money comes from and how it is spent by the candidate" in order to aid the voters in evaluating those who seek ... office. It allows voters to place each candidate in the political spectrum more precisely than is often possible solely on the basis of party labels and campaign speeches. Second, disclosure requirements deter actual corruption and avoid the appearance of corruption by exposing large contributions and expenditures to the light of publicity. In keeping with the purpose of the Act, therefore, disclosure must be made whenever services are provided to a candidate for which full and adequate consideration is not received, since such provision of services is a form of financial support for the candidate. Turning to your specific question, the provision of air transportation for a candidate in connection with his campaign on a corporation's private plane is reportable as a contribution to the extent that the candidate pays less than the fair market value for his flight. In the situation you described, the fair market value is either the maximum allowable charge per passenger under FAA Regulation, Part. 91.181, or the cost of purchasing equivalent service from a commercial airline, whichever is greater. If the candidate pays any less than the fair market value he has received a "discount or rebate not available to the public generally," which constitutes a contribution. Government Code Section 82015. In a subsequent telephone call you also asked what the corporation has to report when its private aircraft is used by a full-time lobbyist employed by the corporation. The travel expenses of the lobbyist should be reported as "other payments to influence legislative or administrative action" under 2 Cal. Adm. Code Section 18620(d). The maximum amount which may be charged under FAA Regulation Part. 91.181 is an appropriate amount to report for the cost of the flight under 2 Cal. Adm. Code Section 18620(d)(2).
